summary: Jeff Atwood explains how he added print and mobile stylesheets to his blog after a reader complained about printing difficulties. He demonstrates that creating a print stylesheet for modern div-based layouts is surprisingly simple, requiring only about 35 lines of CSS to hide irrelevant elements and adjust formatting. He extends this to mobile devices, showing how to test using Windows Mobile emulators. The post serves as both a tutorial and a call to action for web developers to implement these often-neglected features.
key_points: Print stylesheets for div-based layouts are trivially simple - just hide irrelevant elements and adjust margins
Mobile stylesheets are nearly identical to print stylesheets, making them easy to implement together
Testing print layouts is as simple as using File > Print Preview in the browser
The Windows SmartPhone/PocketPC emulator provides a way to test mobile rendering without physical devices
Small usability improvements like print stylesheets are often neglected despite being easy to implement
money_quotes: The entire printer-friendly CSS file is a mere 35 lines including generous whitespace.
Setting up proper print and handheld stylesheets was fun. And easy. I truly regret not doing it sooner.
If you don't have print and handheld stylesheets set up on your website, what are you waiting for?
On a modern site using <div> based layout, optimizing for printers is easy.
The Windows SmartPhone / PocketPC emulator was surprisingly easy to get up and running.
key_insight: Basic web accessibility features like print and mobile stylesheets are so easy to implement that there's no excuse for neglecting them.
